sentences.text: Well, there were many select-- many selections during the stay.
I was-- I was in Birkenau for approximately four weeks.
And in the four weeks, selection is-- there were a number of selections, because a number of transports left Birkenau.
So every time there was a selection, new people came, newer transports came.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We-- the selections we were worried about was the selections when they picked up people who looked weak.
The selection-- oh, the other selections we worried about when we had to go to-- for inspection [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to the-- to the-- to the doctors.
And they looked at our-- our body-- take a closer look at our body, and which maybe wouldn't have been a-- you couldn't have seen it from outside.

And I think mainly the main reason was of these-- of these so-called examinations, which was only people walking through between two tables to spot any sort of illness maybe, probably cont-- contagious in this-- which they didn't want.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there were a number of people-- a number of people didn't return to the-- to the barracks after these inspections.
But one day we knew when we were sent on a transport [? everyone ?] finishes up.
One thing everybody knew is anything was better than staying in Birkenau.
Because the longer one stayed in Birkenau, the chances were getting slimmer and slimmer of getting out of Birkenau alive because the food was absolutely bare necessity.

The circumstances as such, which I'm sure many hundreds of people, thousands of people described before me, were not such that one would have wanted to stay in Birkenau, but mostly because of the-- of the fear of, of the gas chambers and mostly the fear of the selections.
So everyone, everybody wanted to get out.

Just to reflect back for a minute to when I left it off as far as the going to-- getting food, first there were-- the first few days they called volunteers to get the food for everybody in the barrack.
But afterwards, they didn't call for this because nobody wanted to go.
Because what it-- what it consisted of, going for food, we had big tumblers, and which had two, two [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] hooks on.
And the two hooks with, with-- through the two hooks we put through a long stick, a very strong stick, and one on each end was carrying that, that big pot.
It's about that high.
It was very heavy and the soup was very hot, or the food was very hot.
It was always, always soft food-- always soup or some sort of a mash.
We called it in Birkenau, we called it [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] was everything was in it but the, the kitchen sink.

When we got-- when we got to the-- to the kitchen, the reason why we wanted to go to the kitchen is because, I said before, there's always some, some rumors and some news came through about, about families, about who was seen, who was-- who had an unfortunate fate.
Whatever it was, one could hear something else beside the, the regular daily misery.

But what we didn't know when we went for food that Jewish kapos took delight in, in beating our legs while we were carrying the, the hot soups and the hot, hot [NON-ENGLISH] so that anybody who didn't lift his legs exactly as they wanted, they had a fantastic time-- Jewish kapos.
And this is a sad, sad story, that there were kapos here in Sydney who were kapos, and they were never, never exposed.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the result was that either people were, were lifting their legs fast enough, or they-- or they spilled the soup on themselves and they burned themselves.
They scalded themselves.
And it was not one case.
It was quite a few cases.
So after a while, people were just made to go and get the, the, the daily ration.
I'm not saying that it was only Jewish kapos.
There were many kapos.
There were kapos with green triangles who were murderers.
There were the kapos with red triangles who were political kapos.
But our people, they, they were unfortunately not much better.
So, so much for getting the food.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I just forgot what I wanted to say.
Is there another question?
Well, I was just going to ask you about being transferred to the labor camp in Germany.

Well, when, when the time came, by that time when we went to-- when we knew that they were selecting people for a transport, the wor-- the worst thing one could do when it came to family-- father and son or any other relationship-- to try to stay together.
Because the minute they realized that, that, that it was a relationship, they went out-- they definitely went out of their way to separate the people, the family.
For what reason, we don't know, whether it was a sadistic reason or reasons-- this is how it was.
So when-- somehow we knew when it was-- what sort of a selection it [INAUDIBLE] was-- health selection or a transport selection.
When it came to transport selection, we were standing apart from each other.
Not too far, because if it's too far, we could've been cut off.
Because they needed a certain number, and when the number was-- when the number was fulfilled, filled, then they stopped.
So we kept a certain, certain distance with my father from each other, so they're not to be counted as any sort of relatives.
Nobody knew that-- nobody knew who was who.
Nobody knew if it was somebody's brother or sister.
We were only numbers.
And, and so we were lucky-- we were lucky enough to, to manage to get through the health selections and then the transport selection.
And we set out on our journey to Muhldorf.
And I believe we were lucky, because a lot of people were sent from there to Mauthausen.
A lot of people were sent from there to Dachau and worse-- and worse places than Muhldorf.
How much worse, I don't know.
Out of a thousand people when we arrived there on the first week of, of August there were 73 of us left on the end of the war.
So it was-- con-- considering that the-- considering the, the misery, maybe it wasn't the worst.
Considering the, the, the food, considering the, the labor each one had to do, probably it was the worst.
But it couldn't have-- I don't know what the percentage was in other p-- in other camps.
It wasn't a vernichtungslager.
It was an arbeitslager.
That is what the good news was.
That was about the only good news about it.
Yeah, we talked about it.
He talked about it.
Of course he talked about it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We, we talked about [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] every possibility.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah.
We knew what we knew and, and, and we, we tried to, to stay sane.
And, and what kept us going also was we knew that my-- that my mother and my sister were alive when we left Auschwitz.
So one year after Muhldorf, we, we were-- we were put into barracks like any other camp.
I think the barracks were much the same in every camp.
I don't know how many people were in the barrack.
In other camps, we had I think about 120 people to a barrack.
And there was a blockalteste, which was the block elder, and, and a couple of other people who performed the, the-- controlled the, the, the blocks so that everything went in a normal way.
And there was-- in the morning there was an appel and in the evening.
We were taken out to work every morning.
We were marched out to a so-called [GERMAN], which was we were building huge, big hangars for airplanes for, for the Germans, airplane hangars.
I mean I, I don't know exactly the size of it, but they were big enough to, to house an airplane.
And that was-- we built it from, from reinforced concrete.
And part of the job was to-- as the-- as the railway trucks arrived, it was loaded with, with cement bags, which was 50 kilo each.
To unload it, there was a team of approximately 16 people to each-- to each wagon.
And there were 250 bags of cement in every wagon.
So why I know it?
Because believe me, we counted every bag we unloaded.
We counted it many ti-- many times.
So what, what the work was, two people stood-- we'd open the doors.
Two people stood on top of-- on each side of the door, and picked up a bag of cement with their two-- two hands and put it on the back of the person who was standing under the door.
On his back, he was carrying it 50 meters or whatever it took to, to the machine.

And then there was somebody there who had a razor or a knife or something, opened up the bag on one end, and you had to tilt your shoulders and pour it into, into a huge, big cavity there, which after funneled it down to the mixer.
And so by the end of the day, we had to unload one, one truck a day, which, which normally if a person is well-fed and properly fed is not-- between all those people, it's not such a great deal.

But being not fed normally and being on, on a ration of one quarter of a bread-- a loaf of bread is about that big, and then about one fifth of a loaf, and then one sixth of a loaf, because rations were getting smaller and smaller.
It wasn't an easy task to carry 50 kilo.
But the 50 kilo, to carry the bag was easier.
That was the easy part of the job.
Somebody collects this as finished.
There's no, no, no second chance.
It was just taken away and end of the story.
So everybody wanted to make sure that he got home, marched back to the camp, which didn't always happen because they didn't always make it.
But the more unpleasant part of the job was to stand in the truck and hand down, hand down and put it on people's shoulder the bag, because the cement bags are very rough.
They're very-- made from very rough paper.
I don't know if here in Australia-- I haven't touched a cement bag, but neither do I have any intentions, despite of the fact that I'm in the building industry as well.
But cement bags, I don't want to touch.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But the cement bags were, were rough.
And after a few days-- and we saw whoever, whoever, whoever could was really trying to get up on the truck, because he didn't have to carry.
All you have to do is to get the bag of cement and put it onto a person's shoulder.
But after a few days, we had no skin left on our fingers, and there was no medication.
So we very soon found out that, that, that cushy-- that cushion job of not having to carry the bag is not so good after all.
But [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] everybody had to do it because nobody-- you know, there was still some sort of solidarity.
That was somewhat better than it was in Auschwitz.
There was solidarity in the camp, and people were trying to help each other.
I must say it.
When we were together with my father, and we went out to work.
We were marched out to work, and came back from work together, and slept next to each other.
And we were trying to-- we were trying to, to look after each other, naturally like, like any-- everybody else would between a father and son.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Anyway [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what happened was one day my father developed eczema.
And eczema in Germany is called [GERMAN].
That was towards the end of November.
And that was from, from malnutrition.
And also, malnutrition, lack of medication, because nobody in his right mind went to the hospital.
It's called the Lazarette.
Anybody who went to the hospital was really desperate.
So he got an infection, but it was from the bags of cement, from the cement itself.
We were all-- after a while we bandaged our fingers.

[INAUDIBLE] But it was rather, rather risky sort of thing to bandage your fingers to protect yourself, because if you slipped the bag and the bag fell, and it busted open, they just-- you know, it wasn't so pleasant, what, what, what came afterwards.
So he, he developed eczemas and there was-- there was no other way.
There was no other way except to, to take him to the hospital.
There's no way.
Had he lost a lot of weight by now?
Yeah.
Well, probably 30, 35.
Sorry?
You see, my father was 48 when he died.
And he was a-- he was a very tall man.
And he looked-- he looked much skinnier than, than I.
And I wasn't [INAUDIBLE] skinny.
Somehow a young person has got more resilience than an older person, and I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somehow I kept my strength, except my fingers.
I was in a much better condition.
But we had-- we had a doctor in the barrack, Dr.
Lieberman, who was right opposite on the-- on the opposite bed.
And he, he he kept-- he kept bringing us medications.
And he tried to look after us with whatever he had he gave us, but it wasn't to [INAUDIBLE].

When somebody couldn't walk it was-- it-- they, they-- if somebody couldn't walk and couldn't carry the cement, it was out of the possibilities, out of the-- there was no choice but to-- you couldn't stay in the barrack.
You couldn't stay in the barrack, because they dragged you out and they beat you to death.
So you couldn't stay in the barrack.
You had to go to the hospital.
[PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] Yeah.
Yeah, and that was it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was in the hospital for a month.
And, and I went back and he wasn't there.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Yeah.
So in January, I, I had a bit of good luck because from, from Ujpest, we had a family there called Bushonski, and they had a shoe factory.

And in every camp block, Muhldorf as well, there was a, a barrack which was-- we had-- which had shoe repairs, and tailors, and, and also had sort of a warehouse for clothes, because people lost their clothes.
People tore their clothes.
There had to be something.

And I was getting a little bit weak, and one day this-- one of these Bushonski said, next time they want somebody, a tailor or a shoemaker or something, you just-- because they always called out at, at appel, is there a tailor here?
Or is there somebody.
One never knew what they called out for because they called out if some-- is there-- is a musician here?
If they wanted a musician, then they picked the musician to go and clean the, the lavatories, just as a joke.
So anyway, what happened was they called out, is there a tailor around?
So I thought to myself, if I stay much longer out in the-- on the [GERMAN], I won't-- there won't be much left of me.
So I stepped up.
And a fellow looked at me.
Actually it was a kapo-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one of the green, green triangle kapos.
His name was [?
Ruhle. ?] He said, you a tailor?
Oh yes, I said, the whole family.
I spoke fluent German.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Actually it was my second language.
Yeah, I said yeah.
And my full family was, was tailors.
He said, OK, into the [INAUDIBLE] kommando.
From then on, I never went out again to work.
But I got into the-- into the barrack, into the-- so I stayed inside and they covered up for me.
So of course, I couldn't do anything, but I learned to, to put the needle through and whatever it was.
I think that, that's what really saved my life.
And then came one day-- one day we-- they asked for-- actually, they didn't ask.
There were trucks going every couple of weeks to Dachau.
And every couple of weeks what happened was that all the people who, who, who died, there was-- actually, there was two lagers in Muhldorf.
One was the, the [GERMAN] lager, which was-- they didn't have barracks.
They were about five kilometers away from us.
They lived in tents.
And that was-- if anything, that was worse.
But part of our transport went into [GERMAN], and most of the people died there.
Very few people came out alive.
And in the [GERMAN,] it wasn't quite as bad because we were in barracks.
But every couple of weeks, we-- a truck went from Muhldorf to Dachau.
Muhldorf was 77 kilo-- is 77 kilometers from Munich, on one side of Munich.
And the other side of Munich is, is, is Dachau, just outside of Munich.
So they went-- they went with a body full-- with a truck full of bodies to for the crematorium, because they saved them to take it out to the, to the pits.
And on the way back, you filled it up with shoes, and, and clothes, and whatever there was necessary, sometimes food from Dachau.
And one day they just came in to the-- to the workshop and they said, can you spare any people, to the fellow who was in charge.
He said, yeah, we can spare some people.
He knew what we were all about and what was, and a usual, regular trip to Dachau.
And I must tell you that I, I must have been half [INAUDIBLE] already myself because I knew that the truck was loaded with bodies and that some of the bodies was not completely dead.
And the truck was running on, on, on wood.
One of the trucks which, which was-- I don't know what it's called really, but it wasn't on petrol.
It wasn't on, on diesel.
It was the specialty of the German army.
They managed to run trucks on, on wood fuel.
And the exhaust pipe, the exhaust of the truck was coming through on a big-- on a big-- a sort of cylinder inside.
So they could-- they-- when there was no need for it, then it was just an ordinary exhaust pipe.
If there was a need for it, it was-- the truck was locked up and, and went in-- all the gas went into the truck in the back.
It was a gas furnace, yeah.
It was a gas furnace.
So it was three of us went-- with a driver and three of us went to Dachau.
And I just couldn't-- didn't feel anything.
I just went-- I just went for a trip and I was happy to get out of the lager.
And I was-- I think I was even cheerful.
I must-- I must tell you, I don't know-- I don't know what becomes of a person when he knows or maybe saw dead bodies day by day, by dozens.
There was nothing new about somebody being dead.
And this was just an opportunity to get out and not being dead, just this change.
So we got to Dachau.
It didn't take that long.
And within an hour, we were already loading up the, the shoes, the clothes, the whatever it was, boxes, and turned around and went back, back, back to Muhldorf.
And, and I-- afterwards, I really thought to myself, am I this far gone, really knowing what, what we were carrying in the back?
I had no feeling left.
I had no emotions about it.
Absolutely no-- emotionally, I was untouched.
So that was my experience in-- as far as Dachau was concerned.
As far as and well-- That's the end of tape four.
Mm-hmm.
